1: vezot torat ha-asham qodesh qodashim hu (this is the Torah of the guilt offering; it is most holy): the fifth torat formula. The asham is assigned the highest holiness grade (qodesh qodashim). The prominence of this designation reflects the asham's function: it deals with violations that require reparation — the most serious category of inadvertent sin against YHWH's property (holy things) and against neighbor (Leviticus 6:1-7). Its blood goes to the altar as the most holy things; its meat is eaten only by male priests in the sanctuary.
2: bimqom asher yishachatu et ha-olah yishachatu et ha-asham (in the place where the burnt offering is slaughtered, the guilt offering shall be slaughtered): the same location rule as the sin offering (Leviticus 6:25) — the north side of the altar. ve-et damo yizroq al hamizbeach saviv (its blood shall be thrown/sprinkled against all sides of the altar): the blood manipulation for the asham uses zaraq (throw/dash), the same verb as for the burnt offering (Leviticus 1:5) and peace offering (Leviticus 3:2). The blood covers all four sides of the altar — comprehensive atonement in every direction.
3: veet kol chelbo yaqriv mimenu (he shall offer all the fat from it): the fat portions are YHWH's portion in all animal sacrifices. The asham's fat offering follows the same pattern as the peace offering (Leviticus 3): the fat tail, the fat covering the entrails, both kidneys with their fat, and the lobe of the liver are all burned on the altar. Fat (chelev) in Israelite thought is the richest, most nourishing part of the animal — YHWH's portion is always the best (see also the firstfruits principle).
4: ushte hakliayot vehachelev asher aleihen (the two kidneys with the fat on them): the kidneys in Hebrew anthropology are the seat of deep inner life and conscience (cf. Psalms 7:9; Leviticus 16:7; Jeremiah 11:20 — YHWH searches the kidneys). The offering of the kidneys to YHWH symbolizes the consecration of one's deepest interior life, one's most hidden self. veyoteret al hakaved al hakliayot yesirenah (the lobe of the liver, which he shall remove with the kidneys): the liver and kidneys are the internal organs most associated with life and divination in the ancient Near East (cf. Ezekiel 21:21 for pagan liver divination). Their dedication to YHWH asserts that the seat of life and knowledge belongs to him alone.
5: vehiqtir otam hakohen hamizbeach ishah laYHWH (the priest burns them on the altar as a food offering to YHWH): qetoret (burning offering, smoke) — the fat burns into smoke and rises as the divine portion. Ishah laYHWH (‘food offering to YHWH,’ sometimes translated ‘fire offering’ but better as ‘food gift’) is the technical term for the altar's upward portion. The entire sacrificial system uses the metaphor of God receiving a meal — not because YHWH needs food (Psalms 50:12-13: ‘I will not accept a bull from your house or goats from your folds’) but because the metaphor communicates covenant fellowship: the host and guest share a meal.
6: kol zachar bakohanim yochalenah (every male among the priests may eat it): as with the sin offering, the asham meat is restricted to male priests. bimaqom qadosh ye'achel qodesh qodashim hu (it shall be eaten in a holy place; it is most holy): the sanctuary context requirement for qodesh qodashim consumption. The two-fold holiness restriction (gender and location) enforces the graduated holiness system: the closer one is to the divine center, the stricter the requirements.
7: kahatat ka-asham torat achat lahem (like the sin offering, like the guilt offering — one law governs them both): the summary equation of the two most important purification offerings. Despite the distinctions in application (chattat for impurity and inadvertent sin; asham for violated trust and property violations), they share the same procedural law: priestly consumption, most holy status, same-location slaughter. hakohen asher yechapper bo lo yihyeh (the priest who makes atonement with it shall have it): the officiating priest receives the sin and guilt offering meats. This is the economic provision for the priesthood from the atonement offerings — the ministry of reconciliation sustains those who perform it.
8: vehakohen hamaqriv et olat ish or ha-olah asher hiqriv lakohen lo tihyeh (the priest who offers a man's burnt offering shall keep the hide of the burnt offering he offered): the burnt offering is entirely consumed on the altar — but its hide remains. The priest who offers the burnt offering receives the hide. Leather was commercially significant in the ancient world; this provision supplements the priestly income from a portion not itself consecrated to the altar. The detailed listing of priestly portions in Leviticus 7:8-36 reflects the comprehensive economic system supporting the Levitical ministry.
9: vekhol minchah asher te'afeh batanur (every grain offering baked in an oven): three types of grain offering preparation: oven-baked, pan-prepared, and griddle-prepared. All baked grain offerings go to the officiating priest. The variety of preparation methods reflects the practical realities of the ancient kitchen — YHWH's instructions are embedded in real economic and culinary life, not abstracted from it.
10: vekhol minchah belulah vashemen vachaevah lekol bene Aharon tihyeh ish keachiv (every grain offering mixed with oil or dry shall be shared equally among all Aaron's sons): the distribution rule for non-baked grain offerings. Unlike the baked offering (which goes to the officiant), mixed grain offerings are shared equally among all the priests. Ish keachiv (each like his brother) — equal distribution regardless of rank. The priestly fraternity is characterized by material equality even while it has functional hierarchy (high priest over priests over Levites).
11: vezot torat zevach hashelamim (this is the Torah of the sacrifice of the fellowship offerings): the peace offering section. Shelamim (fellowship/peace offerings, from shalom) is the most communal of the offerings: its meat is shared between YHWH (the fat), the priests (portions), and the offerer and family (the rest). It is the covenant meal — YHWH and the offerer eating together. The three subcategories of shelamim are introduced: thanksgiving (todah, Leviticus 7:12), vow offering (neder, Leviticus 7:16), and freewill offering (nedavah, Leviticus 7:16).
12: im al todah yaqrivenhu (if he offers it as a thanksgiving): the todah (thanksgiving offering) is the most urgent shelamim — it must be eaten on the same day (Leviticus 7:15). vehiqriv al zevach hatodah challot matzot (he shall offer with it unleavened cakes): the todah includes a complex bread offering: unleavened loaves mixed with oil, unleavened wafers spread with oil, and fine-flour cakes well mixed with oil — three types of unleavened bread. The abundance of accompanying bread signals the festive, celebratory character of the thanksgiving offering.
13: al challot lechem chametz yaqriv qorbano (along with unleavened cakes, he shall bring leavened bread as his offering with his thanksgiving fellowship offering): the todah uniquely includes leavened bread alongside the unleavened. The presence of leavened bread in the thanksgiving context (though it is never burned on the altar — only the unleavened portions are offered to YHWH) reflects the ordinary bread of daily life joining the sacred feast. The thanksgiving includes the ordinary alongside the consecrated.
14: vehiqriv mimenu echad mikol qorban terumah laYHWH (he shall present one from each offering as a contribution to YHWH): the wave bread (terumah, contribution/heave offering) — one loaf from each type presented as YHWH's portion before being given to the priest. lakohen hazoreq et dam hashelamim lo yihyeh (it belongs to the priest who throws the blood of the fellowship offerings): the priest who performs the blood manipulation receives the wave bread — the officiating minister's portion connects his act to its liturgical reward.
15: uvevar zevach todat shelamav beyom qorbano ye'achel (the meat of the thanksgiving fellowship offering shall be eaten on the day it is offered): the todah must be consumed entirely on the offering day. lo yaniach mimenu ad boqer (none of it may be left until morning): no leftovers from the thanksgiving. The urgency of complete consumption on the offering day reflects the character of thanksgiving: gratitude is immediate, whole, and urgent. What remains unexpressed until ‘another day’ is not gratitude but its deferral. Paul echoes this urgency: ‘giving thanks always and for everything’ (Ephesians 5:20).
16: ve'im neder o nedavah zevach qorbano (if his sacrifice is a vow offering or a freewill offering): two-day consumption for vow and freewill offerings — more relaxed than the todah. The distinction communicates: the vow and freewill offerings express ongoing commitment and spontaneous generosity rather than immediate response to specific divine grace. The different time-frames are fitting: thanksgiving is urgent; ongoing dedication is sustained.
17: vehannotar mivevar hazevach bayom hashelishi ba-eish yisaref (any remaining meat from the sacrifice on the third day must be burned up with fire): the absolute limit on three-day-old meat from peace offerings. The burning prevents the sacred meat from becoming ordinary food. bayom hashelishi (on the third day): the three-day deadline is curious — the same three-day pattern appears in other significant contexts (Hosea 6:2: ‘on the third day he will raise us up’; 1 Corinthians 15:4: ‘raised on the third day according to the Scriptures’). The sacrificial meat that must not remain past the third day is the type of the body that did not remain in the tomb past the third day.
18: ve'im he'achol ye'achel mibesar zevach shelamav bayom hashelishi lo yeratze (if any of the fellowship offering is eaten on the third day, it will not be accepted): lo yeratze (it will not be accepted, not found acceptable) — the technical term for YHWH's rejection of an offering. piggul yihyeh (it is tainted, piggul): piggul (offensive, detestable) — a sacrifice that has gone bad beyond the ordained time. venashea avono (the one who eats it bears his iniquity): tainted-offering consumption is itself a sin requiring atonement. The boundary of legitimate sacrifice is not negotiable by the offerer's convenience.
19: vehavesar asher yiga bekol tamei lo ye'achel (meat that touches any unclean thing shall not be eaten): the contagious impurity principle applied to sacrificial meat. As holiness communicates by contact (Leviticus 6:18, 27), so impurity communicates by contact. Sacrificial meat that has become contaminated must be destroyed. ve-habasar kol tahor yochal et habasar (the clean meat — all who are ritually clean may eat it): the positive rule: clean meat may be eaten by all ritually clean persons, not just priests. The peace offering is the democratic sacrifice — shared broadly.
20: vehanefesh asher tochel basar mizevach hashelamim asher laYHWH vetum'ato alav (if a person eats the flesh of the fellowship offering belonging to YHWH while his uncleanness is on him): the grave offense: eating sacred meat in a state of impurity. venichretah hanefesh hahiv me'ameha (that person shall be cut off from his people): the karet penalty — the most severe sanction in the Levitical system. Karet (‘cutting off’) is debated: it may mean premature death, exclusion from the covenant community, or eschatological judgment. All three dimensions are present: violation of the holiness boundary separates from the community of YHWH's people.
21: venefesh ki tiga bekol tamei (if anyone touches anything unclean): the rule extended to any unclean contact before eating peace offering meat. The multiple categories of uncleanness listed (‘human uncleanness, an unclean animal, or any unclean detestable thing’) cover the full range of contamination sources. venichretah hanefesh hahiv me'ameha: the karet formula repeated — the holiness of the peace offering fellowship meal is not a matter of preference but of covenant membership.
22: A new divine speech formula introducing the fat and blood prohibitions (Leviticus 7:22-27), the fundamental dietary restrictions related to sacrifice. These are addressed to ‘the people of Israel’ — not merely the priests but the whole covenant community.
23: kol chelev shor vechesef ve-ez lo tocheilu (you shall eat no fat — whether from ox, sheep, or goat): the chelev (suet/hard fat, the internal sacrificial fat) prohibition. This fat is YHWH's portion in all animal sacrifice. It is distinguished from shuman (ordinary fat/suet) which may be eaten. The specific fat forbidden is the chelev of sacrificeable animals (ox, sheep, goat) — not fat in general. YHWH claims the richest portion; the prohibition enforces that claim on the whole community, not just during the offering but in all domestic consumption of these animals.
24: vechelev neveilah vechelev terefa ye'aseh lekol melacha (the fat of an animal that dies of itself or is torn by wild animals may be put to other use): the fat of naturally dead (neveilah) or torn (terefah) animals — which cannot be offered — may be used for other purposes (leather-working, lamp fuel, etc.) but not for food. The distinction: YHWH's portion from living animals must not be eaten; the same fat from non-offering animals is not for human food (being still YHWH's in principle) but may be used for other purposes.
25: ki kol ochel chelev min habehemah asher yaqriv mimena ishah laYHWH (anyone who eats the fat of an animal from which a food offering may be made to YHWH): the karet penalty for eating the forbidden fat. The same cutting-off threatened for eating the peace offering in impurity (Leviticus 7:20) applies here — eating YHWH's portion is as grave as eating sacred meat while unclean. The consistency of the karet penalty across multiple violations communicates that the holiness boundary is equally serious wherever it appears.
26: vekhol dam lo tocheilu bechol moshivoteichem (you shall eat no blood of any kind in any of your dwelling places): the blood prohibition is universal — not limited to sacrificial contexts but extending to ‘all your dwellings.’ Blood is prohibited both because it is the bearer of life (Leviticus 17:11) and because it belongs to YHWH as the atonement mechanism. The prohibition applies to birds and land animals — the prohibition extends to the entire animal kingdom available for food.
27: kol nefesh asher tochel kol dam venichretah hanefesh hahiv me'ameha (whoever eats any blood shall be cut off from his people): the karet for blood consumption is stated as an absolute principle in a single sentence. The severity of the penalty reflects the theological weight of the blood prohibition: blood = life = YHWH's atoning mechanism. The one who consumes blood usurps what belongs to YHWH alone and destroys the very mechanism by which atonement is made. The NT explicitly addresses this: the Jerusalem Council retained the blood prohibition for Gentile Christians (Acts 15:20, 29) while the eucharistic ‘drinking the blood of Christ’ (John 6:53-56) operates in a completely different register — Christ's blood is not literally consumed but received by faith.
28: Another divine speech formula introducing the wave and heave portions of the peace offering (Leviticus 7:28-34). These specific priestly portions are the subject of a separate divine command — their importance justifies distinct legislative treatment.
29: medaber el bene Yisrael lemor hamaqriv zevach shelamav laYHWH yavi et qorbano laYHWH mizivcho shelamav (tell the people of Israel: whoever offers his fellowship sacrifice to YHWH must bring to YHWH his own offering from his fellowship sacrifice): the offerer personally brings the wave offering — this is not delegated to the priest. The personal act of bringing the offering before YHWH is the offerer's worshipful gesture, not just a delivery mechanism.
30: yadav teviy'enah et ishei YHWH (his own hands shall bring the LORD's food offerings): the offerer's own hands present the fat and breast before YHWH. et hachelev al hachazeh yavienah lehaniph oto tenufah lifnei YHWH (the fat together with the breast — so that the breast may be presented as a wave offering before YHWH): the tenufah (wave offering) involves a back-and-forth movement before the altar — the offerer presents the portion ‘before YHWH,’ symbolically giving it, then the priest receives it back on YHWH's behalf. The wave motion enacts the giving-and-receiving of the covenant relationship: the offerer gives; YHWH accepts and provides for the priests.
31: vehiqtir hakohen et hachelev hamizbeach (the priest burns the fat on the altar): the fat portion goes up in smoke — YHWH's portion returns to him as fire and smoke. vehayah hachazeh leAharon ulvanav (but the breast shall belong to Aaron and his sons): the breast — the wave offering — becomes the priestly community's portion. The wave breast feeds all the priests, including the high priest's household, distributed as a common meal.
32: ve-et shoq hayamin titenu terumah lakohen (you shall give the right thigh to the priest as a contribution): the right thigh (shoq hayamin) is the second priestly portion from the peace offering — the ‘heave offering’ (terumah, contribution, literally ‘lifted up’). The right side is consistently the privileged side in Israelite culture (cf. the right hand of God as the place of honor, Psalms 110:1). The officiating priest receives the right thigh as his personal portion, distinct from the communal wave breast.
33: hamaqriv et dam hashelamim vehahelev mibene Aharon lo tihyeh shoq hayamin lemanat (the son of Aaron who offers the blood and fat of the fellowship offering shall have the right thigh as his portion): the officiating priest specifically — the one who performs the blood and fat manipulation — receives the right thigh. The connection between the ritual act and its economic provision establishes the principle: ministry and provision are linked. Those who do the work of atonement are sustained by the atonement system.
34: ki et chazeh hatenufa ve-et shoq haterumah laqachti me-et bene Yisrael mizivchei shelameihem (for I have taken the wave breast and the heave thigh from the Israelites' fellowship offerings): YHWH speaks in first person — ‘I have taken’ — then ‘I have given them to Aaron the priest and his sons as a perpetual due from the Israelites.’ The priestly portions are explicitly YHWH's gift: he first accepts them from the people, then redistributes them to the priests. The economy of provision is triangular: people offer to YHWH, YHWH gives to priests through the people's offerings. Paul applies the same logic: ‘those who preach the gospel should receive their living from the gospel’ (1 Corinthians 9:14), citing explicitly the temple precedent.
35: zot mishchat Aharon umishchat banav me'ishei YHWH (this is the portion allotted to Aaron and his sons from YHWH's food offerings): mishchah (‘anointing portion’ or ‘portion’) — the same root as mashiach (anointed one). The portion of Aaron (mishchat Aharon) is a term that connects priestly provision to priestly anointing: they receive their portion because they are anointed for the service of YHWH. Christ as the Anointed One (Mashiach, Christos) receives ‘the name above every name’ (Philippians 2:9) as the priestly-royal share for his completed work of atonement.
36: asher tzivah YHWH latet lahem beyom moshcho otam (which YHWH commanded to be given them on the day of their anointing): the priestly portions date from the ordination ceremony — they are not a later supplement but an original covenant provision. chuqat olam ledoroteichem (a permanent statute throughout their generations): the chuqat olam formula applies to the priestly portions as to all the permanent cultic ordinances. The Levitical priesthood's economic provision is as permanent as the priesthood itself.
37: zot hatorah la-olah laminchah velachatat vela-asham velamiullim velizevach hashelamim (this is the law of the burnt offering, the grain offering, the sin offering, the guilt offering, the ordination offering, and the fellowship offering): a summary formula covering all six major sacrifice types. The six offerings together constitute the complete sacrificial system of the Torah: olah (burnt, vv. Leviticus 6:8-13), minchah (grain, Leviticus 6:14-23), chattat (sin, Leviticus 6:24-30), asham (guilt, Leviticus 7:1-7), miluim (ordination, Lev 8-9), shelamim (peace/fellowship, Leviticus 7:11-36). Hebrews reads this entire list as fulfilled in Christ's one sacrifice: he is the olah (full consecration to God), minchah (pleasing gift), chattat (sin-bearing), asham (reparation), miluim (ordained high priest), and shelamim (peace with God secured).
38: asher tzivah YHWH et Moshe behar Sinai (which YHWH commanded Moses on Mount Sinai): the concluding authority citation — the Sinai origin of the sacrificial laws grounds their divine authority. beyom tzivoto et bene Yisrael lehaqriv et qorbane'hem laYHWH bemidbar Sinai (on the day he directed the Israelites to bring their offerings to YHWH in the wilderness of Sinai): the wilderness setting is theologically significant — the sacrificial system was given during the period of covenant formation, in the liminal space between Egypt and Canaan. The wilderness is the space of total dependence on YHWH where the covenant protocols were established in their purity, before territorial and political complications.
